ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried egg entry (FOODON_03301710); no specific entry exists for fried egg with minced pork. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers fried hen eggs generically. No specific composite code exists for fried egg with minced pork topping. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for fried egg with minced pork as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of fried egg with minced pork is approximately 180 g, comprising one or two pan-fried eggs topped with seasoned minced pork, as commonly served in Malaysian-Singaporean coffeeshops and Bak Kut Teh restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fried egg with minced pork — estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Egg, whole, cooked, fried (FDC ID 173424) and Ground pork, cooked, pan-fried (FDC ID 168351), weighted composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for fried egg with minced pork exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried whole egg (FDC ID 173424) and cooked ground pork (FDC ID 168351) in an approximate 55:45 weight ratio (egg:pork) reflecting typical dish composition. Cooking oil contribution (~5 g per 100 g dish) incorporated into fat estimate. Soy sauce seasoning contributes to sodium estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried egg with minced pork. Eggs are a very-low-carbohydrate food with a GI of approximately 0–10 as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021). Minced pork contributes negligible carbohydrate. Composite dish GI estimated at 8 (low category) based on the egg GI anchor and the near-zero carbohydrate content of the dish (1.2 g per 100 g). Glycemic load per 180 g serving = (8 × 1.2 × 1.8) / 100 ≈ 0.17, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning (~320 mg per 100 g; ~576 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce seasoning and limit portion size, as a single 180 g serving contributes approximately 576 mg sodium, representing roughly 25% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~8)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index produce a negligible glycemic load per serving, supporting postprandial blood glucose control; however, total dietary fat and cholesterol intake should be monitored given the egg and pork fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol (~310 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~4.5 g per 100 g) from egg yolk and pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation; a single serving provides approximately 558 mg dietary cholesterol, exceeding the 300 mg threshold previously cited in clinical guidance, and contributes saturated fat that may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Egg with Minced Pork (肉碎煎蛋, Rou Sui Jian Dan) is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean coffeeshop dish consisting of one or two whole eggs pan-fried in oil until the whites are set and lightly crisped at the edges, topped or surrounded by seasoned minced pork that has been stir-fried with soy sauce, garlic, and sometimes oyster sauce. The dish is savoury, rich, and satisfying, with a contrast of textures between the tender egg yolk, crispy egg white edges, and the juicy, umami-laden minced pork. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice or as part of a Bak Kut Teh meal set. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.8 g per 100 g) and fat (~16.2 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (~1.2 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food suitable for blood glucose management. The egg component provides significant dietary cholesterol, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and choline, while the pork contributes B vitamins, zinc, and iron. Sodium content is moderate to high due to soy sauce seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is not halal due to the pork content. It is gluten-free and dairy-free in its standard preparation.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for fried egg with minced pork was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried whole egg and cooked ground pork in a weighted composite ratio. GI was estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database egg reference. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in USDA SR Legacy and the composite estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried whole egg (FDC ID 173424) and cooked ground pork (FDC ID 168351) in an approximate 55:45 egg-to-pork weight ratio, with cooking oil contribution incorporated. No direct entry for this composite dish exists in regional primary databases (HPB Foodo, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): eggs and plain minced pork contain no dietary fibre; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source data for both component foods. 3. GI is estimated at 8 (low) by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database egg reference; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 4. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal. 5. Sodium estimate (~320 mg per 100 g) incorporates a typical soy sauce seasoning contribution; actual sodium will vary by restaurant preparation. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g reflects naturally occurring trans fats from pork fat (vaccenic acid); not from industrial hydrogenation. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 8. The food_name in the input ('B6 Fried Egg 肉碎煎蛋') uses a menu-style restaurant code prefix ('B6'); the canonical English name has been normalised to 'Fried Egg with Minced Pork'. 9. cuisine_tags in the input included 'Soup' which is not applicable to this dish; this tag has been disregarded as it likely reflects the parent restaurant category (Bak Kut Teh) rather than the dish itself.
